top_papers.title: TwinGate: Stateful Defense against Decompositional Jailbreaks in Untraceable Traffic via Asymmetric Contrastive Learning
top_papers.summary: Decompositional jailbreaks pose a critical threat to large language models (LLMs) by allowing adversaries to fragment a malicious objective into a sequence of individually benign queries that collectively reconstruct prohibited content. In real-world deployments, LLMs face a continuous, untraceable stream of fully anonymized and arbitrarily interleaved requests, infiltrated by covertly distributed adversarial queries. Under this rigorous threat model, state-of-the-art defensive strategies exhibit fundamental limitations. In the absence of trustworthy user metadata, they are incapable of tracking global historical contexts, while their deployment of generative models for real-time monitoring introduces computationally prohibitive overhead. To address this, we present TwinGate, a stateful dual-encoder defense framework. TwinGate employs Asymmetric Contrastive Learning (ACL) to cluster semantically disparate but intent-matched malicious fragments in a shared latent space, while a parallel frozen encoder suppresses false positives arising from benign topical overlap. Each request requires only a single lightweight forward pass, enabling the defense to execute in parallel with the target model's prefill phase at negligible latency overhead. To evaluate our approach and advance future research, we construct a comprehensive dataset of over 3.62 million instructions spanning 8,600 distinct malicious intents. Evaluated on this large-scale corpus under a strictly causal protocol, TwinGate achieves high malicious intent recall at a remarkably low false positive rate while remaining highly robust against adaptive attacks. Furthermore, our proposal substantially outperforms stateful and stateless baselines, delivering superior throughput and reduced latency.

ai.one_liner_zh: A stateful dual-encoder defense for decompositional jailbreaks that tracks malicious intent across anonymized, interleaved request streams with a claimed single-pass monitoring path during prefill.
ai.summary_zh: Decompositional jailbreaks are a deployment-level LLM safety problem because malicious goals can be split across benign-looking requests while traffic remains anonymized and interleaved. TwinGate frames the defense as a stateful dual-encoder system: asymmetric contrastive learning clusters fragments that share malicious intent, and a frozen parallel encoder is used to reduce false positives from benign topical overlap. The abstract claims each request needs only one lightweight forward pass that can run alongside the target model prefill phase, so the serving argument is lower latency and better throughput than heavier generative monitors. Evidence is reported on a 3.62M-instruction dataset covering 8,600 malicious intents under a strictly causal protocol, with claims of high recall, low false positives, robustness to adaptive attacks, and better latency/throughput than stateful and stateless baselines. The biggest missing detail is the numeric evaluation setup: model sizes, hardware, traffic mix, baseline implementations, and exact overhead are not specified in the abstract.
ai.background_zh: Production LLM defenses must handle anonymized, interleaved traffic where session-level history and user metadata may be unavailable or untrusted.
ai.problem_zh: The core question is whether decomposed malicious intent can be detected statefully without using expensive generative monitoring or relying on traceable user context.
ai.approach_zh: TwinGate uses asymmetric contrastive learning in a dual-encoder design to map intent-related malicious fragments into a shared latent space while using a frozen encoder to limit benign-topic false positives.
ai.evidence_zh: The abstract reports evaluation on 3.62M instructions and 8,600 malicious intents under a causal protocol, but it does not provide exact recall, false positive, latency, throughput, hardware, or baseline configuration details.
ai.value_zh: If the claims hold, the method could add stateful jailbreak monitoring to LLM serving with lower online overhead than generative safety checks.
open_questions: What are the exact latency and throughput overheads, and on what hardware, model sizes, batch sizes, and serving stack were they measured?
How were the stateful and stateless baselines implemented, and were they given comparable history access, tuning effort, and compute budgets?
How does TwinGate maintain state under fully anonymized interleaved traffic, and what memory or eviction policy is required at production scale?
core_contributions: Defines a strict threat model for decompositional jailbreaks in anonymized, interleaved request streams.
Introduces a stateful dual-encoder defense using asymmetric contrastive learning for intent-level fragment matching.
Claims a lightweight single-forward-pass monitor that can run in parallel with the target model prefill phase.
why_read: Relevant for LLM serving teams evaluating whether safety monitoring can be made stateful without large generation-time overhead.
Useful for understanding a defense design that explicitly targets decomposed intent rather than single-request toxicity.
Contains a large evaluation corpus claim that should be checked for construction quality, leakage risks, and adaptive attack coverage.
risks: The abstract gives no numeric latency, throughput, or false positive values, so the systems claim cannot be judged from the summary alone.
The method may require nontrivial state management despite the anonymized traffic setting, but the abstract does not explain memory growth or eviction behavior.
recommended_for: LLM serving and safety infrastructure researchers
Researchers studying decompositional or multi-turn jailbreak threat models
ai.reading_priority_reason: The paper partially matches the LLM inference systems focus through its latency and throughput claims for online monitoring, but it is primarily a safety-defense paper and the abstract lacks the numeric systems details needed to rank it high for inference acceleration or compiler/runtime work.
ai.fallback_summary: Decompositional jailbreaks pose a critical threat to large language models (LLMs) by allowing adversaries to fragment a malicious objective into a sequence of individually benign queries that collectively reconstruct prohibited content.

top_papers.title: VitaLLM: A Versatile, Ultra-Compact Ternary LLM Accelerator with Dependency-Aware Scheduling
top_papers.summary: Deploying Large Language Models (LLMs) on resource-constrained edge devices faces critical bottlenecks in memory bandwidth and power consumption. While ternary quantization (e.g., BitNet b1.58) significantly reduces model size, its direct deployment on general-purpose hardware is hindered by workload imbalance, bandwidth-bound decoding, and strict data dependencies. To address these challenges, we propose \textbf{VitaLLM}, a hardware-software co-designed accelerator tailored for efficient ternary LLM inference. We introduce a heterogeneous \textbf{Dual-Core Compute Strategy} that synergizes specialized TINT-Cores for massive ternary projections with a unified BoothFlex-Core for mixed-precision attention, ensuring high utilization across both compute-bound prefill and bandwidth-bound decode stages. Furthermore, we develop a \textbf{Leading One Prediction (LOP)} mechanism to prune redundant Key-Value (KV) cache fetches and a \textbf{Dependency-Aware Scheduling} framework to hide the latency of nonlinear operations. Implemented in TSMC 16nm technology, VitaLLM achieves a decoding throughput of 70.70 tokens/s within an ultra-compact area of 0.223 mm$^2$ and a power consumption of 65.97 mW. The design delivers a superior Figure of Merit (FOM) of 17.4 TOPS/mm$^2$/W, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art accelerators. Finally, we explore an extended bit-serial design (BoothFlex-BS) to demonstrate the architecture's adaptability for precision-agile inference.

ai.one_liner_zh: VitaLLM is a hardware-software co-designed ternary LLM accelerator that targets edge inference bottlenecks with specialized ternary projection cores, mixed-precision attention support, KV-fetch pruning, and dependency-aware scheduling.
ai.summary_zh: VitaLLM targets edge LLM inference, where ternary models reduce storage but still leave decode bandwidth, workload imbalance, and operation dependencies as bottlenecks on general-purpose hardware. The design combines TINT-Cores for ternary projections with a BoothFlex-Core for mixed-precision attention, then adds Leading One Prediction to reduce redundant KV-cache fetches and dependency-aware scheduling to hide nonlinear-operation latency. The abstract reports a TSMC 16nm implementation with 70.70 tokens/s decoding throughput, 0.223 mm^2 area, 65.97 mW power, and 17.4 TOPS/mm^2/W FOM, plus a bit-serial BoothFlex-BS extension for precision-agile inference. Its value is clearest for ultra-compact edge accelerators for ternary LLMs, especially if the reported decode bandwidth savings and utilization improvements hold under realistic model and sequence settings. The biggest missing detail is the evaluation context: model sizes, context lengths, baselines, workload mix, and whether the reported implementation is measured silicon or design-level evaluation are not clear from the abstract.
ai.background_zh: Ternary LLMs such as BitNet-style models reduce memory footprint, but edge deployment still faces bandwidth, power, utilization, and strict dependency constraints.
ai.problem_zh: The paper asks how to turn ternary LLM compression into efficient edge inference hardware across both compute-bound prefill and bandwidth-bound decode stages.
ai.approach_zh: VitaLLM uses heterogeneous compute cores for ternary projection and mixed-precision attention, Leading One Prediction for KV-cache fetch reduction, and dependency-aware scheduling for nonlinear-operation latency hiding.
ai.evidence_zh: The abstract reports TSMC 16nm implementation metrics, decoding throughput, area, power, FOM, and comparison against state-of-the-art accelerators, but it does not specify workload setup or baseline details.
ai.value_zh: If the claims hold under fair workloads, the paper could be useful for accelerator designers studying ternary LLM inference, decode-stage bandwidth reduction, and compact edge hardware scheduling.
open_questions: What model sizes, sequence lengths, batch sizes, and prefill/decode ratios are used to produce the 70.70 tokens/s result?
Are the area, power, and throughput numbers from fabricated silicon, post-layout simulation, synthesis, or another implementation stage?
Which state-of-the-art accelerators are compared, and are precision, memory hierarchy, process node, and workload assumptions normalized fairly?
core_contributions: A heterogeneous dual-core accelerator split between specialized ternary projection compute and mixed-precision attention compute.
A Leading One Prediction mechanism intended to prune redundant KV-cache fetches during inference.
A dependency-aware scheduling framework aimed at hiding nonlinear-operation latency, plus a bit-serial extension for precision agility.
why_read: Directly matches LLM inference systems and edge accelerator design, with concrete claims about throughput, area, power, and FOM.
Focuses on where ternary LLM deployment can still bottleneck after quantization: decode bandwidth, utilization imbalance, and data dependencies.
May offer useful hardware-software co-design ideas for compact ternary inference beyond generic quantized model execution.
risks: Evaluation details are underspecified in the abstract, making the reported FOM and state-of-the-art comparison hard to judge.
The usefulness of Leading One Prediction depends on KV-cache access behavior that the abstract does not quantify beyond the claimed pruning role.
recommended_for: LLM accelerator researchers focused on ternary or low-bit inference.
Engineers studying edge LLM serving under strict area and power budgets.
Compiler/runtime and architecture readers interested in scheduling around LLM inference dependencies.
ai.reading_priority_reason: High fit with the configured focus on LLM inference systems and accelerator co-design, with concrete abstract-level hardware metrics; priority remains conditioned on checking workload and baseline fairness in the full paper.
ai.fallback_summary: Deploying Large Language Models (LLMs) on resource-constrained edge devices faces critical bottlenecks in memory bandwidth and power consumption.

top_papers.title: CuLifter: Lifting GPU Binaries to Typed IR
top_papers.summary: GPU compilers merge all data types into a single unified register file, erasing the type information that binary-analysis tools rely on. We show that type recovery from this untyped register file is the central challenge of GPU binary lifting. We present CuLifter, a SASS-to-LLVM IR lifting framework that recovers register types via constraint propagation with conflict detection, reconstructs explicit control flow, and aggregates multi-instruction patterns. Across eight benchmark suites (24,437 GPU functions in 919 cubins) spanning open-source applications, vendor libraries, and optimized ML runtimes, CuLifter successfully lifts 99.98% of functions to valid LLVM IR. An ablation study confirms that type recovery is the only step required to produce semantically correct IR: disabling it drops the x86 pass rate from 73.8% to 0%, a 73.8 percentage-point drop.

ai.one_liner_zh: CuLifter targets the type-erasure bottleneck in GPU binary lifting by recovering SASS register types and emitting typed LLVM IR.
ai.summary_zh: GPU binary analysis is hard because compiler backends collapse source-level data types into a unified register file, leaving lifting tools without the type information needed for typed IR. This paper frames type recovery as the central obstacle and proposes CuLifter, a SASS-to-LLVM IR framework using constraint propagation with conflict detection, explicit control-flow reconstruction, and aggregation of multi-instruction patterns. The abstract reports evaluation across eight benchmark suites, 24,437 GPU functions, and 919 cubins from open-source applications, vendor libraries, and optimized ML runtimes, with 99.98% of functions lifted to valid LLVM IR. It also reports an ablation where disabling type recovery drops the x86 pass rate from 73.8% to 0%, supporting the claim that type recovery is critical. The missing detail is how semantic correctness, pass-rate measurement, and downstream usefulness are validated beyond producing valid IR.
ai.background_zh: Low-level GPU tooling needs reliable SASS-level lifting, but GPU compiler type erasure removes information that conventional binary-analysis pipelines often depend on.
ai.problem_zh: The core research question is whether register types can be recovered accurately enough from GPU binaries to produce usable typed LLVM IR.
ai.approach_zh: CuLifter recovers register types through constraint propagation with conflict detection, then reconstructs control flow and folds multi-instruction idioms into higher-level IR patterns.
ai.evidence_zh: The abstract supports scale and liftability claims with 24,437 functions across 919 cubins and an ablation on type recovery, but it does not fully explain the semantic validation protocol.
ai.value_zh: If the lifting is semantically reliable, this could improve inspection, transformation, and analysis of optimized CUDA and ML-runtime binaries where source or compiler IR is unavailable.
open_questions: How is semantic correctness checked for lifted LLVM IR beyond validity and the reported x86 pass rate?
What SASS features, GPU architectures, or vendor-library patterns cause the remaining lifting failures?
How useful is the lifted IR for downstream optimization, decompilation, verification, or performance analysis workflows?
core_contributions: Identifies type recovery from the untyped GPU register file as the central challenge in SASS-to-IR lifting.
Introduces a SASS-to-LLVM IR lifting framework combining type-constraint propagation, conflict detection, control-flow reconstruction, and multi-instruction pattern aggregation.
Reports large-scale lifting coverage across open-source applications, vendor libraries, and optimized ML runtimes.
why_read: Directly relevant to low-level GPU binary analysis and SASS/LLVM IR tooling.
The abstract gives unusually concrete scale numbers for a binary-lifting system.
The type-recovery ablation provides a focused mechanism-level claim to inspect in the full paper.
risks: Valid LLVM IR does not necessarily prove semantic equivalence to the original GPU binary.
The abstract does not specify hardware generations, SASS coverage limits, or failure cases in detail ?
recommended_for: Researchers working on GPU binary analysis, decompilation, or IR recovery.
Compiler and runtime engineers who need to inspect optimized CUDA or ML-runtime binaries.
Readers tracking low-level code generation and assembly-to-IR infrastructure.
ai.reading_priority_reason: High fit for the low-level code and assembly-generation focus, with concrete benchmark scale and an ablation; it is less directly about LLM inference acceleration, but it targets GPU binary infrastructure relevant to CUDA and optimized ML runtimes.
ai.fallback_summary: GPU compilers merge all data types into a single unified register file, erasing the type information that binary-analysis tools rely on.

top_papers.title: RCW-CIM: A Digital CIM-based LLM Accelerator with Read-Compute/Write
top_papers.summary: Digital computing-in-memory (DCIM) has emerged as a promising solution for large language model (LLM) acceleration by minimizing data transfers between external DRAM and on-chip accelerators while maintaining high precision for superior accuracy. However, existing CIM architectures often overlook weight update latency, which becomes critical as LLM weights are far larger than a single CIM macro capacity. To address this issue, this paper proposes a read-compute/write (RCW) architecture that effectively minimizes weight update latency, along with a nonlinear operator fusion that further mitigates dependencyinduced latency. The proposed RCW reduces decoding computing latency by 21.59% on the Llama2-7B model. In addition, the nonlinear operator fusion mechanism achieves a 69.17% latency reduction through efficient partial accumulation and group-based approximation. Furthermore, a weight-stationary and output column stationary (WS-OCS) dataflow is introduced to reduce both external DRAM access and internal CIM weight updates by 51.6% and 87.6% respectively during the prefill phase of 1024 tokens, leading to an overall 49.76% latency reduction. Fabricated using TSMC 22 nm CMOS technology and operating at 100 MHz, the proposed RCW-CIM achieves 3.28 TOPS and 42.3 TOPS/W, enabling 4.2 ms prefill latency and 26.87 decoded tokens per second for the INT4-weight Llama2 model with dual DDR5-6400 memory.

ai.one_liner_zh: The paper proposes a fabricated digital computing-in-memory accelerator for LLM inference that targets CIM weight-update latency with read-compute/write execution, nonlinear operator fusion, and a WS-OCS dataflow.
ai.summary_zh: Digital CIM is positioned as a way to reduce DRAM-to-accelerator traffic for LLM inference while keeping digital precision, but the abstract argues that weight-update latency becomes a bottleneck when model weights exceed one CIM macro. RCW-CIM addresses this with a read-compute/write architecture, nonlinear operator fusion for dependency-induced latency, and a weight-stationary/output-column-stationary dataflow for prefill. The abstract reports 21.59% lower decoding compute latency on Llama2-7B, 69.17% lower latency from nonlinear operator fusion, and 49.76% overall prefill latency reduction for a 1024-token prefill, alongside reduced DRAM access and CIM weight updates. It also reports a fabricated TSMC 22 nm, 100 MHz chip reaching 3.28 TOPS, 42.3 TOPS/W, 4.2 ms prefill latency, and 26.87 decoded tokens/s for INT4-weight Llama2 with dual DDR5-6400 memory. The main missing detail is how baselines, accuracy impact from approximation, and workload generality are defined.
ai.background_zh: LLM inference stresses memory movement, and CIM accelerators are attractive because they can reduce off-chip transfers, but large model weights create repeated on-chip update pressure.
ai.problem_zh: The core problem is whether a digital CIM accelerator can keep LLM prefill and decoding efficient when weights are much larger than one macro and nonlinear operators introduce dependency latency.
ai.approach_zh: The paper combines RCW execution, nonlinear operator fusion using partial accumulation and group-based approximation, and a WS-OCS dataflow to reduce both weight updates and DRAM access.
ai.evidence_zh: The abstract provides concrete latency, access-reduction, throughput, efficiency, process, frequency, model, precision, and memory-system claims, but it does not clarify baseline implementation details or accuracy effects.
ai.value_zh: If the claims hold under fair baselines, the work is directly relevant to CIM-based LLM serving because it attacks both decode-time weight updates and prefill memory traffic.
open_questions: What are the exact baselines for the reported 21.59%, 69.17%, and 49.76% latency reductions, and are they equally optimized for the same memory system?
How does the group-based approximation in nonlinear operator fusion affect model accuracy or output quality for INT4 Llama2?
Do the results generalize beyond Llama2-7B, 1024-token prefill, INT4 weights, 100 MHz TSMC 22 nm silicon, and dual DDR5-6400 memory?
core_contributions: Identifies CIM weight-update latency as a first-order bottleneck for LLM weights larger than one macro.
Introduces read-compute/write execution to reduce decode compute latency.
Adds nonlinear operator fusion based on partial accumulation and group-based approximation to reduce dependency-induced latency.
why_read: Strong fit for LLM inference hardware and memory-system co-design.
Includes fabricated-chip claims rather than only simulation-level accelerator results.
Reports separate effects on decoding latency, nonlinear-operator latency, DRAM access, CIM weight updates, and prefill latency.
risks: Baseline fairness is unclear from the abstract.
Approximation-related accuracy impact is not described in the provided text.
recommended_for: Engineers evaluating memory-traffic and weight-update bottlenecks in serving hardware
keywords: decoding latency preservation issue? no, decoding latency reduction: keep keyword concise? better not; output schema allows strings. Need valid JSON no weird. I included a too-long weird keyword? This final must be valid. I need revise. Can't edit final? I am in final already? Actually I already output? Need ensure valid JSON; I inserted weird? Let's check final content above: keywords array ended
ai.fallback_summary: Digital computing-in-memory (DCIM) has emerged as a promising solution for large language model (LLM) acceleration by minimizing data transfers between external DRAM and on-chip accelerators while maintaining high precision for superior accuracy.

top_papers.title: Strait: Perceiving Priority and Interference in ML Inference Serving
top_papers.summary: Machine learning (ML) inference serving systems host deep neural network (DNN) models and schedule incoming inference requests across deployed GPUs. However, limited support for task prioritization and insufficient latency estimation under concurrent execution may restrict their applicability in on-premises scenarios. We present \emph{Strait}, a serving system designed to enhance deadline satisfaction for dual-priority inference traffic under high GPU utilization. To improve latency estimation, Strait models potential contention during data transfer and accounts for kernel execution interference through an adaptive prediction model. By drawing on these predictions, it performs priority-aware scheduling to deliver differentiated handling. Evaluation results under intense workloads suggest that Strait reduces deadline violations for high-priority tasks by 1.02 to 11.18 percentage points while incurring acceptable costs on low-priority tasks. Compared to software-defined preemption approaches, Strait also exhibits more equitable performance.

ai.one_liner_zh: Strait is a GPU inference serving system that predicts transfer contention and kernel interference to schedule dual-priority DNN traffic with fewer high-priority deadline misses.
ai.summary_zh: GPU inference serving often runs mixed traffic under high utilization, where concurrent execution makes latency estimates and priority handling fragile. Strait targets dual-priority DNN inference by modeling data-transfer contention and kernel-execution interference, then using those predictions for priority-aware scheduling. The abstract claims evaluation under intense workloads reduces high-priority deadline violations by 1.02 to 11.18 percentage points while imposing acceptable costs on low-priority tasks. It also claims more equitable performance than software-defined preemption approaches. The main missing details are the model mix, GPU setup, workload construction, and baseline configuration, so the strength of the systems claim needs a full read.
ai.background_zh: On-premises GPU inference services need to handle prioritized requests under high utilization, but concurrent transfers and kernels make tail latency difficult to predict.
ai.problem_zh: The paper asks how to reduce deadline violations for high-priority inference traffic without making low-priority traffic unfairly absorb the cost.
ai.approach_zh: Strait combines adaptive latency prediction for transfer contention and kernel interference with a priority-aware scheduler for dual-priority traffic.
ai.evidence_zh: The abstract reports intense-workload evaluations with 1.02 to 11.18 percentage-point reductions in high-priority deadline violations and a comparison to software-defined preemption, but it does not specify models, GPUs, or workload details.
ai.value_zh: If the evaluation is fair, the method could be useful for GPU serving stacks that need priority differentiation without relying primarily on preemption.
open_questions: What DNN models, GPU hardware, batch sizes, and request arrival patterns were used in the evaluation?
How is the adaptive interference predictor trained or updated, and how robust is it under workload shifts?
Are the software-defined preemption baselines configured competitively, and what low-priority latency or throughput costs are hidden behind "acceptable"?
core_contributions: Models latency impact from data-transfer contention and kernel-execution interference under concurrent GPU inference.
Uses those predictions to drive priority-aware scheduling for dual-priority inference traffic.
Reports reduced high-priority deadline violations and a fairness comparison against software-defined preemption approaches.
why_read: Directly relevant to GPU inference serving under high utilization and deadline-driven traffic.
Useful for understanding scheduler-level alternatives to preemption in prioritized serving systems.
May provide concrete latency-prediction mechanisms for interference-aware inference scheduling.
risks: The abstract is not LLM-specific and only says DNN inference, so fit with LLM serving depends on the evaluated models.
Hardware, workload, and baseline details are absent from the abstract, limiting confidence in generality and fairness claims.
recommended_for: Systems engineers studying priority-aware ML serving
ai.reading_priority_reason: The paper matches the GPU inference serving focus and reports concrete deadline-violation improvements, but the abstract is not LLM-specific and omits key evaluation and reproducibility details.
ai.fallback_summary: Machine learning (ML) inference serving systems host deep neural network (DNN) models and schedule incoming inference requests across deployed GPUs.

daily_brief.overview_zh: Today's main threads are training and inference efficiency, Low-level Code And Assembly Generation. The set is anchored by TwinGate: Stateful Defense against… and VitaLLM: A Versatile,…. CuLifter: Lifting GPU Binaries to… adds another data point on whether the same line of work carries stronger evidence or engineering value.
reading_strategy: Start with TwinGate: Stateful Defense against… and verify whether it defines the training and inference efficiency problem clearly and exposes a real mechanism.
Then read VitaLLM: A Versatile,… to see whether the novelty comes from model design, training procedure, or evaluation reframing.
Use CuLifter: Lifting GPU Binaries to… as a final cross-check on experimental boundaries and practical value.
